data.title: FCOM: An Overlooked Dividend Growth Opportunity With Big Tech Exposure
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Fidelity MSCI Communications Services Index ETF (NYSEARCA:FCOM) deserves a buy rating due to the fund’s hybrid growth and income opportunities that are not yet fully appreciated by the market. While many of FCOM’s top holdings are tech companies that have generated significant capital appreciation, another overlooked quality is its dividend growth potential. History has shown that top dividend growers have delivered strong risk-adjusted returns particularly compared to companies that offer no dividends, making FCOM relatively undervalued.

Fidelity MSCI Communications Services Index ETF – Overview and Compared ETFs

FCOM is a passive ETF that tracks an index consisting of U.S. communications companies including media, interactive gaming, and advertising. With its inception in 2013, the fund has 92 holdings and $1.69B in AUM. The fund is cap-weighted and uses a representative sampling strategy. I wrote previously on the fund last year and still rate the ETF a buy for the reasons discussed in this article.

For comparison purposes, other funds examined are State Street Communication Services Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLC), Vanguard Communication Services ETF (VOX), and iShares Global Comm Services ETF (IXP). XLC aims to capture comm sector holdings within the S&P 500 Index. VOX is a passive fund that also captures the comm sector and is the heaviest on interactive media, entertainment, and integrated telecommunications. IXP is included for comparison because it captures a global view of communication services and captures large, mid, and small cap companies.

FCOM: Performance, Expense Ratio, and Dividend Yield

FCOM has seen a 10-year average annual return of 10.64%. The Fidelity fund has outperformed both VOX and IXP, which had 10-year average returns of 8.54% and 8.82%, respectively. XLC has seen an average annual return of 11.84% since its 2018 inception. All funds underperformed the S&P 500 Index over the past 10 years. This underperformance is predominantly because high-growth companies like Meta Platforms (META) and Alphabet (GOOGL), (GOOG) were not added to the communications sector until 2018.

While FCOM has experienced strong capital appreciation, I believe an underappreciated quality is the fund’s dividend growth. FCOM has seen a 3-year dividend growth CAGR of over 25%. Although the fund has only a 0.95% yield currently, FCOM’s top holdings have significant dividend growth capacity which will continue to fuel dividend growth in coming years. The fund also has a very affordable 0.08% expense ratio.

FCOM Holdings: Strengths and Outlook Factors

FCOM’s top holdings include META, GOOGL, GOOG, Netflix (NFLX), and Verizon (VZ). This composition is starkly different from a decade ago when the sector was dominated by telecom and traditional media instead of its current mega-cap holdings. Other funds have a similar mix of holdings. However, XLC includes Live Nation Entertainment (LYV) and IXP, the only global comm fund, also includes Tencent Holdings (TCEHY).

Almost 50% of FCOM’s weight is in Meta Platforms, Alphabet, and Netflix. While this presents concentration risk, I believe Meta and Google will significantly grow their dividends in coming years. Additionally, I believe Netflix has long-term optionality to initiate a dividend, increasing the attractiveness of each of FCOM’s top-weighted holdings.

Meta Platforms: Offering a Blend between Potential Income and Growth

You may not think of Meta Platforms as a significant dividend provider. However, I believe the company is positioned for stable, dividend growth in coming years. A key factor for this claim is Meta’s daily active user count which reached a record 3.58B at the end of last year. More importantly, Meta’s average revenue per person reached $16.56 in Q4 2025, representing another record. This steady growth, along with the company’s low dividend payout ratio of just 7.1%, puts Meta Platforms in a prime position for dividend growth. The company announced its first-ever dividend back in February 2024 and its forward dividend yield currently stands at just 0.33%. However, given its consistent revenue, stable user count, and low payout ratio, I believe Meta is prime to increase its dividend yield in the coming years.

Capital expenditure for Meta also saw a huge increase between 2024 and 2025, reaching $72.2B in 2025, up from $39.2B. This capex is about to be translated into products including Meta Quest 4, expected to be released later this year. A key feature for the Quest 4 is eye tracking, which puts the product on par with the Apple Vision Pro, costing over $3,000 compared to the Meta Quest 4 at only $800. Therefore, Meta will continue to hold a competitive advantage in the virtual reality market, offering investors a strong blend of capital appreciation and income potential.

Alphabet: Significant Subscriptions Growth will Drive a Higher Dividend Yield

The second key strength for FCOM is its weight in Google. The largest growth segments for Alphabet have been Google Search and subscription services as both segments saw 17% YoY growth. These segments have translated into a steady source of revenue for the company, and 22% YoY increase in operating income. Google also saw 750 million Gemini App monthly active users and 325 million paid consumer subscriptions across platforms like YouTube.

2026 will be a key year for Google as it looks to potentially double its capital expenditure on AI-driven infrastructure like data centers and servers. This spending will ensure that Google remains on the leading edge of search monetization as other competitors like Microsoft (MSFT) are also seeing heavy AI investment. Even with competitive pressures, Google’s net income margin of 32.8% and dividend payout ratio of just 7% mean that the company has significant dividend growth potential. Although its current dividend yield stands at just 0.26%, I believe Alphabet’s steady revenue growth from subscriptions and search will make it a prime dividend grower over the next decade.

Netflix: Strong Consumer Loyalty and Content Investment will Perpetuate FCF Growth

A key challenge for companies like Netflix stems from consumer loyalty. This is because steady revenue for companies like Netflix is reliant on customers and viewers that stick with these platforms. However, digital service companies reported low consumer loyalty so far in 2026. Loyalty programs and incentives showed only limited results. Thankfully, for companies like Netflix, subscribers are relatively exclusive with 80% not also subscribing to competitor services.

However, this loyalty relies on consistent quality products and content. Like Meta and Google, Netflix has the capacity for both growth and dividend initiation. For example, its spending on upcoming content hit a record high last year, totaling $18B, compared with $16.2B in the previous year. The company’s 16% YoY revenue growth and 24% net income margin have translated to a 17% YoY levered FCF growth. Therefore, while Netflix's leadership has made no plans to initiate a dividend in the near term, I believe the company has both the competitive advantages and free cash flow to initiate a dividend within the next decade.

Why Dividend Growth Matters

So, why should investors care about dividend growth? In a 40-year study, companies within the S&P 500 Index that grew their dividends saw higher total performance compared to those companies that did not pay or did not grow their dividends. FCOM’s top holdings represent companies that demonstrate strong dividend growth and therefore historically outperform non-dividend payers.

As we have seen in the past, FCOM’s top holdings could change. High-growth companies with little to no dividend yield could replace holdings like Meta and Google. However, I believe these companies will maintain dominant positions in the coming years and investors will benefit significantly from their dividend growth.

Risks to Investors

Aside from the concentration risk previously discussed, another key risk to my thesis and for investors is the impact of long-term dividend growth at the expense of capital appreciation. Verizon, FCOM’s #5 holding at 4.8% weight, has increased its dividend for 20 consecutive years. In doing so, the fund has a current yield of 5.9% with a dividend payout ratio of 58%. As a result, the company has less capacity for capital expenditure and growth. Consequently, revenue growth has been relatively flat for the past five quarters.

Should FCOM’s top holdings expand their dividends too quickly, the fund risks low growth at the expense of income generation. Therefore, while dividend growth is an important quality, too much of something can still be a bad thing. Investors seeking purely dividends are likely to be disappointed in overall returns due to traditionally lower capital appreciation at the expense of a high dividend yield.

Current Valuation and Outlook

FCOM has outperformed most peer funds and the S&P 500 Index over the past year. A key driving factor is Google’s recent performance including a 107% one-year share price return. This is predominantly due to the shifting view of Google which was previously thought to be threatened by ChatGPT.

Despite FCOM’s strong recent performance, the ETF still has a favorable valuation compared to the rest of the U.S. market. For example, the P/E ratio for the S&P 500 Index stands at nearly 30x. Due to FCOM’s current valuation and its blend of capital appreciation and dividend growth potential, I believe FCOM will outperform both its long-term average and the S&P 500 over the next couple years.

Concluding Summary

Historically, the communications sector has underperformed the broader market. However, this changed when growth-focused companies like Meta and Google became classified within the sector. While these companies are focused on strategic reinvestment, I believe they are also demonstrating strong dividend growth potential. This potential represents a strong outperformance signal and therefore a buy rating for FCOM. FCOM’s specific holdings mix, low expense ratio, and current valuation give the fund an edge over peer comm sector ETFs.

data.title: First Advantage: Upgrade To Buy On Improved Fundamentals As Valuation Stayed Depressed
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on First Advantage (FA), which I recommended a hold rating for due to my expectation that H2 2025 was likely to be a weak quarter and also that the macro situation was not positive, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. I am now turning bullish because FA has shown it can still grow even while base demand remains slightly negative. FA should see healthy growth ahead given the strong commercial traction, solid retention, and a much more proven Sterling integration.

FA no longer needs a hiring rebound to work

First of all, FA has shown us that it can deliver solid performance while base demand is still slightly negative. For reference, Q4 revenue was up 12% y/y on a pro forma basis, and adj. EBITDA was up 17% y/y. This makes me a lot more bullish than before because FA's equity story doesn't need a hiring rebound to work. The underlying growth drivers are actually levers that FA can control. For instance, the new logo and cross-sell momentum were strong, which tells me the combined platform is gaining traction with customers. Retention also stayed solid, which matters because it suggests the Sterling integration is not creating disruption. In other words, this quarter showed that FA can still grow through execution even when the broader backdrop is not in its favor.

There is one caveat, though. Part of the Q4 growth came from some customers waiting to go live on FA's platform and holding screening volume back from the previous provider. In other words, there was some delayed demand that came to fruition, and management said that likely created a couple of points of timing shift between Q3 and Q4. This is not necessarily bad news, but it just means Q4 should not be treated as the new clean run rate. That said, I am not worried about the momentum slowing. Combined new logo, upsell, and cross-sell growth was 17% in Q4, and FA booked 17 enterprise deals in the quarter, bringing the 2025 total to 66. These were meaningful wins, with each worth at least $500,000 of expected annual contract value [ACV]. On this point relating to enterprise, new business was up even stronger than the broader business at 24% y/y, with average deal size up by high double digits, and notably, deals are becoming more bundled and more complex. Lastly, management also said the late-stage pipeline was near record highs.

All these data points improve the quality of FA's equity story. The clearest implication, in my view, is that bigger deals and more bundled wins suggest customers are trusting FA with a broader part of the workflow. That should make those relationships stickier and more valuable over time, and it also supports the Sterling acquisition logic. We can see that in the cross-sell trends as well. Package density is going up as customers add more screening products, and I think the reason is straightforward. Employers are increasingly focused on risk. Management even pointed to survey data showing strong intent to add more screening products over the next one to two years. With Sterling, FA now has an expanded product portfolio to capture more of this demand.

Sterling

More on the point regarding the Sterling deal, I think the integration story now looks even more solid than before. One of the most important developments here is that FA completed the core elements of the Sterling integration in 2025, and they did it without impacting customer experience. The KPI to track here is not how much cost synergy FA has extracted but customer behavior, and by that, I mean retention rate, which stayed at ~96% for the year and even improved to 97% in H2 2025. In my view, that says two things. First, the migration appears to have been handled cleanly, without the kind of service disruption that usually causes customers to leave during integrations. Second, it suggests the combined platform is holding customer trust well enough for FA to build on those relationships, which is important because this feeds into the broader cross-sell and revenue synergy story (making it more believable). Remember that FA did all these while the synergy side of things proceeded well. FA has already extracted tangible cost benefits, with now a run rate of $55 million as of year-end 2025.

Another very encouraging sign is that management said some customers came back after trying competitors. This is perhaps one of the most important data points because it suggests the combined platform is much better than when Sterling/FA was a standalone entity, and it is good enough for customers to choose it again.

Valuation

Put together all the above, and I think the story has shifted to bullish. Last year, the big question was whether FA could finish the integration without disrupting the base business. We are obviously past that now, with FA moving into 2026 with the core integration work largely done, retention still strong, and synergies still coming through. The growth outlook is also bright, and hence, I now believe FA's stock is cheap. Most importantly, the stock is now trading at 8.7x forward PE, the lowest ever in the past 10 years. The disparity between an improving earnings growth outlook (refer to the consensus estimates chart below) and a worsening valuation multiple doesn't make sense to me. We can debate on what multiple FAs should trade, but if we say that FAs can achieve consensus. FY27 adjusted. EPS of $1.4 and multiply that by 11x NTM PE (this is the -2 standard deviation level, by the way), the stock is easily worth ~$15.4.

Risk

As I said, some parts of Q4 benefited from delayed go-lives that shifted volume into the quarter, so growth could look softer as that normalizes. I also think that if FA leans more into buybacks, the balance sheet may not deleverage fast enough to ease investors' concern about the current leverage level. Of course, there is always the risk that the macro turns even worse, and even if FA can execute well, it would be dragged down by the overall market.

Conclusion

I am upgrading FA to a buy rating. FA has shown it can drive growth through execution, even without much help from the broader hiring environment. Sterling integration looks solid, retention remains strong, and the combined platform is clearly helping FA win larger and broader customer relationships. With the business improving while the stock still trades at a depressed valuation, I think the risk-reward has shifted positively.

data.title: Duos Technologies: My Conviction Hasn't Shifted
data.description:
Investment Thesis

We now have a trilogy, as this is the third time I’m covering Duos Technologies Group, Inc. (DUOT). The company continues to operate under the radar of most investors and the market. With the most recent earnings report covering Q4 and FY2025, the trend is clear: DUOT is a hypergrowth company looking to scale its unique data center model. The recent funding helps ensure these investments actually come to fruition as well. Even though this has taken a toll on the stock price, seeing as it was done through equity, the upside is still plentiful in my opinion.

I’m also updating my revenue projections for the company, seeing as it’s now beginning to look like it will work on even larger projects than the small-scale pods, potentially being a major catalyst for the top line. I’m sticking with a Strong Buy for DUOT.

FY2025 Report—Strong Growth All Around

The FY2025 report came out on March 31, 2026, and since then, the stock has remained largely flat. Since my first coverage, the returns have been far from good, but that is something I warned about as well. This is a long-term play, and downturns in the range of 30-40% will happen. I’m still invested at an average cost of $10.2 per share, so I’m in the same boat as most people reading this article, too. For Q4, the top line and bottom line missed both, but given the upbeat guidance for 2026, I think it helped mute many of the otherwise negative aspects in terms of financial figures.

Despite the miss, the revenues have grown a staggering 271% YoY compared to 2024. Much of this comes from “services and consulting—related parties.” For those not aware, these are revenues that DUOT gets from an asset management agreement with New APR Energy, signed back in late 2024. It’s not here to stay, but it has helped bridge capital for DUOT to invest more aggressively in deploying its network of pods. Because this deal expires this year, there will be a revenue cut to anticipate.

Turning to our 2026 outlook. The company is providing revenue guidance of $50 million to $55 million in total revenue across all business lines.

Guidance, on the other hand, is a different beast. It implies that growth might slow down from 2025 but still post 94% YoY growth based on the mid-range.

New Contract Changes The Game

This contract is expected to generate approximately $176 million in revenue over a 36-month term, with margins exceeding 80%

When removing the $22 million impact of the asset agreement, actual revenues for 2026 would be closer to $30 million instead, showing a YoY growth rate closer to 10%. But what I really don't want to miss pointing out here is the new revenue service that is helping replace this agreement, namely GPU-as-a-service. It’s something I’ve written about before when I covered Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS). That is a large-scale data center company, but the business model is the same: it rents out GPUs, the same as what DUOT will do.

This revenue is often determined by the speed and capacity of the models used. With 2,304 GPUs, each would be generating about $76,388 in revenue per unit. But here's the kicker: DUOT will own these GPUs thanks to a recent funding round of $65 million. This is considerable, considering the market cap sits around $200 million right now.

The partnership is fully funded through the Company's recently completed $65 million public offering and hardware financing arrangements already in place, ensuring deployment can commence immediately without reliance on additional equity financing.

But because of the recent funding, DUOT cites that no additional equity financing would be needed to start this deal with Hydra Host. This definitely reduces the downside risk because it means DUOT is ready to go, and the heavy dilution we saw recently won't be a common occurrence or something to expect in the near term.

There's no official statement on this, but assuming $65 million, which is the raised capital from earlier, gets you 2,304 NVIDIA GPU B800s, the average cost would be $28,211 per unit. Going back to my earlier estimate for the revenue per unit ($76,338), DUOT would report 170% higher revenue compared to the purchase price.

Going back to my earlier coverage on some hyperscalers like NBIS and NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), one of the main discussions there is the lifespan of GPUs. Most companies seem to account for a useful lifespan of 4 years right now. This means DUOT could get another year of revenues from its GPUs. Of course, the revenues might be lower. At 30% of the average revenue over 36 months, it's still $53,471 per unit, or $123 million in revenues.

Change In Leadership

With the last report, there was also the announcement of a new CEO for the company, Doug Recker. He is taking on the post as CEO following Chuck Ferry, who won’t be leaving the company entirely but will remain on the board. It was in 2024 that Recker came into DUOT after having successfully sold a data center company after having founded it in 2017. There doesn't seem to be any public disclosure for the sales price, but I did see that in 2020, the company got an investment of $30 million. So the scale is likely somewhat similar to where DUOT is now.

When he came into DUOT, he was appointed to lead the DUOS Edge AI. So that he now gets tapped as CEO is a clear tell, I think, about where DUOT is heading as a company, or wants to at least. Normally, you’d see a company fall sharply in value with an announcement like this, but because it came with a very big band-aid in the shape of the $176 million contract, the reaction was very muted. Overall, I think it was a very good decision for the company and another way of showing that it’s stepping away from the legacy business and into data centers fully. Doug has a long history in the industry, and with a successful past, I think this will only bring more benefits to the company (and, in turn, shareholders).

Updated Revenue Outlook

In the first article I wrote about DUOT, I forecasted revenues of $32.5 million this year based on a pretty optimistic 65 units overall. The actual revenues should come in lower but still show the ARR that DUOS could report. My first article came out last year, and there I looked at only the EDS pods. Of course, DUOT reported much higher than the $7.5 million I estimated. But it was another way of gauging what would be the core business, seeing as the Asset Agreement generating more of the revenues is ending this year.

There haven’t been any changes to the unit targets, meaning DUOT is still looking at 150 deployed by 2028. To go back to the Q2 2025 call, the revenue per unit is about $400,000 per year on the mid-range. This implies $75 million in revenues from this part alone in 2028.

So by 2028, DUOT might post upwards of $133.6 million in revenues when combining the EDC pods and the new revenue stream. This puts the company at an FWD P/S of just 1.49x in 2028 and 2.19x this year. Why this consolidated figure is so much higher than the management guidance is because I've used the ARR for the EDS pods and spread out the GPU-as-a-service the same over 3 years. Firstly, DUOT won't reach 65 pods suddenly, but over the course of the year, I think they will. So by the end of 2026, ARR for those should be $32.5 million, based on the mid-range guidance of what management has said each one would generate.

The deal with Hydra Host also won't really commence instantly and likely will scale up. This means that in 2027 and 2028, the actual revenue contributions will be much higher than this year. Still, I think my model is a way to see the scaling potential of the company at the moment and what actually lies ahead.

There aren’t many forecasts out there for the company; most point to $40 million this year, which I think is very inaccurate, just based purely on what the management has guided for. But for comparison, the sector median is 3.32x FWD sales. This implies that shares have an upside of 51.5% in the next 12 months and 122% over the next 3 years. In both cases, the return is incredibly attractive right now, and what makes it better is that DUOT is fully capitalized to make this happen as well. It’s not hard to maintain a Strong Buy rating for the company when this is the outlook right now.

Risks

The main risk for DUOT is that the profit margins for the new GPU-as-a-service fail to materialize fully. If they come in below estimates and the guidance issued, I think we’ll see the stock trend even lower. A lot hinges on whether DUOT can successfully bring in capital from this and scale the business without having to resort to more equity financing. If that happens, I think the risk premium would have to go up, and my Strong Buy might go to a Buy or even a Hold, depending on the dilution rate.

With $120 million in expected EBITDA, it’s crucial that DUOT can reinvest this into a new set of GPUs and scale further. Because that figure doesn't account for depreciation in the GPUs, the actual net income might be closer to $23 million–$25 million instead, assuming a viable lifespan of 4 years, leading to D&A costs of $16.2 million per year. That is not small enough to ignore. It also means that the valuation right now is about 10x FWD earnings.

With G&A costs still very high and rising quickly, I think the market will be looking towards DUOT managing to ensure a consistently profitable bottom line from this agreement and not risk having a ballooning operating expense pressure it into the negatives.

The Full Picture

DUOT has been coming up with some very strong guidance for the year and fantastic news about new partnerships, which in turn also opens up a new revenue stream for the company. A CEO change might make most companies stumble in the short term; the strong outlook posted helped mute most of this.

I’m updating my revenue outlook for the company, pointing to an over 100% return over the next 3 years, should it be valued as the sector based on sales. With the investments in GPUs being fully capitalized after the last funding and there not being a need for more, DUOT can now enter a phase of organic growth where rising profits get reinvested to scale both the EDC pods and the new GPU-as-a-service. I’m sticking with a Strong Buy rating for the company.

data.title: Oil Crisis Shifts Players, Prices, And Security Quest
data.description:
The price of oil is a vital economic proxy for the health of the global economic order, the one price to rule them all. There's still virtually no other price that speaks volumes about the state of global economies and geopolitics than that of oil. Now, we're reminded that the price of oil is global and set by the global market. The U.S. produces roughly 22-25% of oil and gas supplies globally. The magnitude of this Middle East crisis scathes all markets and countries, to greater and lesser degrees, based on their supply-demand fundamentals and export-import dynamics.

Some Fundamentals

The U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) laid out its case for the rest of the year.

In March, production of 7.5 million barrels per day (b/d) of crude oil production was shut in. April is projected at 9.1 million b/d of shut in production, assuming the conflict does not persist past April and that traffic through the Strait of Hormuz gradually resumes. Under those assumptions, shut-ins will fall to 6.7 million b/d in May and return close to pre-conflict levels in late 2026.

OPEC recently highlighted that production has declined by 7.56M barrels per day to 22M in March owing to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

In this best-case scenario, the EIA says production resumes at full supply in late 2026, with global oil prices forecast at $96. As a top-producing oil and gas country now, the Dallas Fed found that oil prices in 2018 would have been roughly 36% higher had the shale revolution not occurred. They projected that the shale revolution showed a reduction in current oil price volatility of around 25%, with long-run volatility reduced over 50%. For all of the estimations, no one quite predicted such a cascade of effects and countermoves, leading to a large and unique cocktail of volatility.

Interestingly, in an energy symposium last week, a top analyst from the Middle East highlighted how OPEC’s management of spare capacity offered the globe a $200 billion value, i.e., reducing volatility because of the spare capacity buffer. There has only been about 1-1.5 million barrels per day (m/b/d) of spare capacity surrounding the crisis timeframe. Recently, Dallas Federal Reserve Bank economists developed scenarios of how the duration of the crisis increases prices, understandably. The magnitude of the price impact is based on the length of outages and the amount held off the market.

They find (quoting the report):

  • outage that only lasts one quarter would raise the average WTI price to $110 per barrel in April 2026;
  • an outage lasting two quarters would cause the WTI price to peak at $132 per barrel in July 2026;
  • and an outage lasting three quarters would cause the WTI price to peak at $167 in October of 2026.

Why the Shut-Ins?

The Fed writes:

“Oil producers in the Gulf shut in many of their oil wells as the oil could not be stored or exported. Lack of storage capacity is why many oil producers in the region started curtailing their production in early March 2026, particularly Iraq and Kuwait. Other were attacked that forced reductions (like Saudi Arabia).

Oil demand is not peaking anytime soon. Crude oil is expected to peak near 112 million barrels per day (m/b/d) in 2050, according to some analyst groups. The year 2025 ended with a supply of 106 m/b/d. It's to decline 2 m/b/d in 2026, owing to the crisis effects, supply disruptions, and demand curtailment. Several countries in Southeast Asia have begun rationing, supply diversifications, and other workarounds. More will follow as countries address energy security concerns and rising prices.

New Routes

Just over a year ago, a sizable decline in oil prices was leading to a different outlook for U.S. oil and gas, one of peak supply concerns. Up to 2024, the U.S. had been producing 22% of the crude and liquids market and 26% of natural gas. This was a herculean supply-side feat. Production in the path was even thought to be peaking in 2027 or 2026. Yes, inflation is occurring in producing the resources, but one rig can drill what two rigs drilled five years ago, according to a producer from the symposium.

Basically, 20% of global supply moves through the Strait of Hormuz. Other key transit routes are picking up new cargoes, such as Panama and the Cape of Good Hope. The U.S. is expected to export more crude next month, much of it to Asia. With roughly 20% of Qatari LNG throttled back, more LNG will come from the U.S.

Prices, Prices, Prices

Price discovery of other goods is tied to the price of oil, too. Discussions of U.S. peaking oil supplies are a misnomer. Price and technological advances matter greatly in the next 5-10 years.

While many focus on the WTI/Brent futures outlook, the spot market has said otherwise. The Dubai oil basket has been most relevant to Asian markets, as it's the pricing for the 80% of the market that originates from the Persian Gulf. The spot price reached $170 at one point, according to the Saudi analyst from the symposium. This shows the impact of the physical shortages, not the expectations built into the futures markets, as these charts above show.

Importantly, futures markets can be disconnected from the spot market that is traded and shipped. This fact was evident across the years in both pre- and post-pandemic times.

Below, I'm looking at some of the trends that show the impact from a year and one-month viewpoint. The S&P is inching up slightly. Matador (MTDR) has ridden the upward slope.

The price of oil is a vital economic proxy for the global economic order. The Dallas Fed modeled the shock based on direction of closure. The severity of inflationary effects is worse as outages drag on.

The Fed focused on inflation expectations too, as they are what drive the behavior of firms and consumers. They follow the same general idea as the projections, but expectations are more baked in and stickier than actual projections of what would occur. A three-month closure takes about two quarters to level off.

The authors say:

“At one-year expectations rise by between 0.4 and 0.8 percentage points at their respective peaks, and 5-10 year expectations increase by at most 0.09 percentage points.”

The effects subside in the early months of 2027.

Investment Implications

The basic takeaway from the crisis is that oil and gas matter in a portfolio. These “value-oriented” stocks and funds are proving more valuable. The curve is moving upward and extending outward relative to last year’s trades. Midstream firms, like Energy Transfer (ET), Williams (WMB), and Enterprise (EPD), are often a safe way to play too, with stable dividends or distributions, plus they will benefit from exports with their assets near the Gulf Coast. With the volatility in QQQ, my energy portfolio reduced the declines - the former FAANG (now the Mag 7) to FANG (Diamondback proxy) weightings. Semiconductors have continued to do well.

What comes next will be a scramble to restore and regain market share and restore stability. While oil markets are trying to assess the risk premium, the volatility that shale helped curtail in oil markets is back. My bets are that $100 oil stays for a while.

data.title: Cedar Grove Capital Management Q1 2026 Letter
data.description:
“This too shall pass.”

April 10, 2026

To all clients,

Cedar Grove Capital Management’s Multi-Strategy Composite returned -23.0% (net of fees) vs -5.6% for the Russell 2000, -5.9% for the Russell Microcap, and -5.5% for the S&P 500, since its inception.

CGCM Multi-Strategy Composite 1,2,3
(All Managed Portfolios with Balances >$20,000)
Net Return Russell 2000 Russell Microcap S&P 500
YTD through March 31, 2026 4 (23.0%) (5.6%) (5.9%) (5.5%)

1) Time-weighted returns (TWR) are unaudited and represent an account opened during the reported quarter. Investor's actual returns may differ from the returns presented due to when onboarding was completed and position weightings with each respective portfolio.

2) Portfolios consist of client accounts that are strictly involved with our multi-strategy offering (L/S + Special Situations).

3) Standard fees consist of a 1.0% management fee, applied monthly on the end balance, as well as a 20% performance fee above our Russell 2000 benchmark, calculated at the end of the year. A high-water mark is standard across all accounts and is measured over the entire life of the clients' investment.

4) YTD represents the composite inception date of January 14th, 2026 through the end of the quarter March 31, 2026.

- Past performance is not indicative of future results.

This is our first quarterly letter since launching our multi-strategy approach to investing via separately managed accounts (SMA) in the middle of January. We’ve thought long and hard about how to start this letter, and we think providing some background is helpful. Leading up to our formal launch, we had the pleasure of speaking to and hearing from other portfolio managers who started at various points of the last few cycles.

While we all know that timing plays an important role in the early success of funds, the fact is that we cannot forecast exogenous shocks that begin during turbulent macroeconomic times. Rather than opine on macro uncertainties, the best we can do is invest in good companies at reasonable prices with solid prospects to compound. Take, for example, one portfolio manager we spoke with who launched in January of 2020. We all know how that panned out, and he ended up being down 30% by the end of Q1 and needed to explain to LPs that the sky wasn’t falling. The point we’re making is that timing from unforeseen events can get the better of us, at least in the near term. But, just like all recent past events that have led many investors to run for cover (2018 Fed policy, COVID, and 2022 inflation), they too have all eventually passed and led the markets to new highs.

For us, launching into a SaaS apocalypse this year almost felt like a blessing. Many of the names we liked were selling off in what seemed to be a healthy pullback in their respective valuations, despite improving fundamentals and being AI beneficiaries rather than being disrupted by AI. Better put, the companies we liked were being thrown out with the bathwater, and we were eager to take advantage.

As we took note of falling equity valuations, we deployed capital with the expectation that the improving fundamentals of our holdings would be reflected in Q4’25 earnings and the market would be quick to rectify their selloffs once fears around guidance would be rendered overblown.

On that end, we were right. Nearly all the names we held that reported earnings in February or early March reported well, and we were quite happy with their respective guides. However, while we were quick to deploy funds to capitalize on these dislocations, we were quickly surprised by Trump launching a combat operation against Iran. Flashbacks to the summer of 2025 with a quick in and out operation and no Strait closure seemed like a re-run of the same playbook all over again. Unfortunately, this was not the case, and all the good results that our holdings released just days earlier were quickly forgotten, and any remaining holdings that reported in the rest of March popped on results and were quickly sold off within the broad market selloff.

An event that we not only a) did not foresee, and b) misinterpreted by how decisively the IRGC would respond by closing the strait. As we've highlighted many times, we are not traders nor will we ever pretend to be. It's important to understand one's edge in investing and ours revolves around finding mispriced equities with supporting tailwinds. We feel that trading around a market that no one really knows where it will go on any given day based on a tweet is a recipe for disaster and just creates more problems than it solves. Because of that, we have refrained from becoming emotional in these uncertain and tumultuous times and stuck with our investment philosophy that managers will have to be able to stomach painful drawdowns to avoid trying to time the market as long as they believe that the companies they hold are of quality.

We believe that we've found many quality names with good management teams and strong tailwinds that are being thrown out along with many other small and microcap companies in what seems to be a "shoot first and ask questions later" environment. Additionally, while our multi-strategy approach allows us to take short positions, our mandate does not require us to consistently hold short positions. We strategically take short positions in companies we feel are overearning, facing intense or looming headwinds, or making questionable operational business decisions that will eventually become negatively reflected in their stock price. We do not take positions just to reduce our overall beta exposure, resulting in us generally maintaining a long bias.

Our special situations sleeve, which is also opportunistic in nature, indirectly insulates our portfolio by reducing our portfolio beta. There were a number of holdings that more or less stayed within a range and did not contribute as much to the downside as others in our overall performance. However, there were a few names in that strategy that were large detractors for us in the quarter, which we will also highlight below.

In summary, it's slightly poetic that we spent so much time in preparation for our launch, talking to industry leaders and other managers who started in what looked to be clear skies, only for us to innocently fall into a similar chaotic market situation as they did. But, just like all the tough market environments of the past, despite even facing a generational pandemic, blue skies did eventually come back.

While the timing of macro events got the better of us in Q1, going into Q2, we feel confident in the names that we hold and that their future execution will be accurately reflected in our performance in the coming quarters and years.

Below, we're going to highlight our holdings with a quick explanation of each. If you have any questions or would like to discuss further, you can email us below.

Best,

Paul Cerro, Founder and CIO of Cedar Grove Capital Management

When it came to deploying capital, we largely stuck within consumer discretionary and various healthcare names, including some biotech. We understand that the hottest trades in the market over the last few years have been anything AI-related, but we've chosen to look elsewhere for mispriced assets. This was done by design as fewer eyeballs were on smaller companies that were either currently in an inflection period in their cycle or were pressing forward with exploiting operating leverage. In other words, while we acknowledge that there is plenty of money to be made in AI, we left the chasers to chase the "hot new thing" while we focused on where our edge is.

Our biggest contributors of the quarter were Evolv Technologies (EVLV), Nektar Therapeutics (NKTR), and OneSpaWorld (OSW). Our biggest detractors were WW International (WW), The RealReal (REAL), and KITS Eyewear (KITS.TO).

Realized Holdings

OneSpaWorld (OSW)

OSW is one of the very few names that we revisit from time to time as more of a trade than an investment for us. OSW is a spa operator that largely operates its business on various cruise lines (Carnival Cruise, Norwegian, etc). The reason we trade this name from time to time is that it still consistently gets misunderstood by the market in times of turmoil that mainly affect cruise lines directly, rather than OSW — think rising oil prices or a reduction in consumer discretionary spending.

While we haven't shared any deep dive into the company specifically, last year we did share our high-level thoughts here on why the dislocation regularly occurs.

Hims & Hers Health (HIMS)

If you've known us for even a little bit, you've most likely known our coverage of this name from a bearish point of view since January 2025. Our continued notes on the name last year, on the company relying heavily on their compounded GLP-1 offering to offset a decelerating core business, allowed for multiple opportunities to realize gains on the short side. Needless to say, Andrew (CEO) eventually did fly too close to the sun and was inevitably sued for IP infringement by Novo Nordisk (NVO) in February of this year. A big psychological win for us. On the day of earnings, we entered into our latest short position in the company and quickly realized gains the following morning on questionable guidance and earnings call hype than reassurance.

We have not had any other position with the company since, but we did highlight the potential drawback of HIMS shift away from GLP-1s going forward.

LENSAR (LNSR)

LENSAR was a merger arbitrage trade that was going on for almost the better part of a year. Having approved an offer from Alcon (ALC) for $14/share last March, the deal kept getting pushed back but was still within the window outlined in the original merger agreement. Despite what would be a large share of the femtosecond laser-assisted cataract surgery (FLACS) market, a new FTC under the Trump administration seemed to be pro-merger, especially after allowing the Live Nation (LYV) suit to be settled in a way that largely benefitted the company.

However, not even a month after extending the merger, LNSR announced that the deal was being terminated. We exited that position the day after the announcement, but we're still optimistic that the company could get a second chance at life once management gets back on track.

We shared more about those thoughts here and will keep a watchful eye in the future if an opportunity presents itself again.

WW International (WW)

We've been highlighting the opportunity at WW after they emerged from bankruptcy as a leaner weight loss play on the booming GLP-1 market. With >$1 billion of their debt erased from bankruptcy proceedings, the trade here was for WW's conservative forward estimates to represent a good entry point while the transformation into a medication-forward business took shape. We were pleased with just how much their clinical business was estimated to grow in Q1'26 due to the successful launch of the Wegovy Pill (+54% sequential growth), but we were very surprised by the sequential decline in behavioral subscribers during peak season, which has never happened during normal market circumstances as far as we know. Due to risk management decisions, we decided to heavily reduce our position in WW after this announcement and officially exited the position when Tara announced she stepped down as CEO once her employment contract ended on March 31st.

We had high hopes for this company and still think that it could work, but given the market environment we're in and Tara leaving, we concluded that there were better risk/reward opportunities to deploy capital into.

You can read our most recent note after Q4'25 earnings here , which was prior to Tara stepping down.

Core Positions

KITS Eyewear (KITS.TO)(KTYCF)

KITS is a vertically integrated Canadian-based DTC contact and eyeglass company that does business in both Canada and the U.S. The CEO is having his "second act" with KITS after selling his first optical company (Coastal Contacts) in 2014 for ~$430 million CAD. Unlike other DTC-native companies like Warby Parker (WRBY), KITS started its business by selling low-margin, but high-retention contacts instead of eyeglasses. This allowed them to create a sticky business while slowly building out the higher-margin eyeglass business, all while avoiding building out a capital-intensive retail presence. This has allowed them to grow to $200M CAD in LTM sales in just eight years. With a net cash position, positive adjusted EBITDA, and good capital allocation, we feel that KITS will be able to compound its business for years to come.

You can read our original note here as well as our most recent follow-up .

The RealReal (REAL)

The RealReal is a domestic omnichannel luxury resale retailer that is capitalizing on the second-hand fashion movement that is taking the U.S. by storm. While many investors are worried about traditional luxury retailers facing various headwinds (LVMH just had their worst year since the GFC), and the value-oriented consumer getting squeezed from the opposite end, REAL gets misunderstood as falling into one or both of those categories. We feel like this understanding is incorrect, and REAL operates in its own unique category (second-hand luxury) that is growing almost twice as fast as regular luxury (11.6% vs 5.2%).

Even though REAL operates in its own category, the company's business model only works if it's able to expand its supply in order to drive more demand. A delicate balance between the two. REAL has already done well to unlock further supply from customers across the country (tariff-free), which has driven higher gross merchandise value (GMV), revenue, and adjusted EBITDA. With the help of AI being able to increase throughout for product authentications and listings, operating leverage has started to show. The company is once again guiding for a double-digit topline growth year with ~200bps of adjusted EBITDA margin expansion in FY'26. With strong tailwinds, growth in GMV, and margin expansion, REAL should be able to capitalize on the massive consumer shift to second-hand merchandise and the benefits of AI.

You can read our original note here as well as our most recent follow-up .

Evolv Technologies (EVLV)

Mass shootings are unfortunately becoming more commonplace in the U.S. these days, with the number through the end of March 31st already up 35% y/y. EVLV aims to help with that by providing various customers (arenas, government buildings, hospitals, educational centers, etc) with AI-integrated hardware that can detect different forms of firearms and knives before they can become a threat. EVLV has >1,000 customers and deployed >8,000 systems to-date. The company operates via hardware + software (SaaS) while allowing customers to either buy the hardware outright with a lower subscription contract or a lower hardware price with a higher subscription contract. This mix shift has led the company to end FY'25 with >$120 million in ARR (+21.3% y/y), inflecting positive on a cash flow basis and sitting on a net cash pile (~$40 million). Once again, we are bullish companies with strong tailwinds, and while, unfortunately, the tailwinds here are mass shootings, they don't seem to be coming down anytime soon, which allows EVLV to continue compounding.

You can read our original note here as well as our most recent follow-up .

Sanuwave Health (SNWV)

Sanuwave Health is a medical device company in the wound care space. They help patients who have open wounds — such as ulcers — to speed up the process by using a non-contact ultrasound device called 'UltraMist.' The procedure is largely covered by insurance but has historically operated in an industry where plenty of Medicare fraud occurs (skin grafts). In Q3'25, CMS dropped the hammer on the skin graft fraud by limiting how much could be covered under the old way of doing things. This put pressure on the stock, given the uncertainty on reimbursement rates or changes to CPT coverage codes could affect the business. SNWV got the clarity needed and is now able to take advantage of the changing landscape in its favor going forward. This is on the heels of a highly successful debt refinancing in September of last year, along with a better-than-feared Q4'25, in which other competitors saw deteriorating results. SNWV is small, but has a proven product, a long runway in the wound care space now that CMS guidance has been officially updated, and the CEO looks open to potentially a buyer coming in at some point as an additional call option.

We shared our notes and a podcast interview last year on the name, if you're interested in learning more.

The Oncology Institute (TOI)

The Oncology Institute is a niche oncology platform that delivers end-to-end cancer care for lower-cost outpatient areas. The business strategy runs on a value-based care (VBC) model, where it assumes risk and is paid for managing total patient outcomes rather than just a standard billing per service model. This allows TOI to align incentives across payers, physicians, and patients, lowering the total cost of care while capturing economics across the full oncology value chain. The stock performance has done well since bottoming in 2024, with new management implementing changes that have directly led to accelerated topline growth via new payer contracts and rapid growth in the pharmacy segment. The company is currently inflecting towards an adjusted EBITDA profitability and is proving that operating leverage can be achieved as their VBC model continues to gain traction.

Special Situations

Abivax (ABVX)

Abivax is a small French biotechnology company that released P3 data in July of last year on its ulcerative colitis (UC) drug, obefazimod. Obefazimod appears to be a better option for treating UC than JAK inhibitors or TNF-alpha blockers, given its efficacy, delivery, tolerability, and safety profile. Plenty of rumors have come from French publication La Lettre about a possible buyout of the company from various pharma companies looking to boost their existing UC portfolio. Given the limited number of good drug assets in the market, Abivax seems like an excellent take-out target ahead of maintenance data later this summer. Rumors have circulated that Abivax could fetch between $15 and $20 billion before maintenance data is released. If no deal comes through, commercialization will come next, which is already being prepared with their recent CCO hire. Assuming maintenance data comes in positive, the future looks bright for Abivax, whether the strategy is to get acquired or bring the drug to market.

You can read our original note on the opportunity here .

Nektar Therapeutics (NKTR)

Nektar is another biotechnology company with a drug asset, rezpegaldesleukin (“Rezpeg”), that primarily targets atopic dermatitis (AD, or eczema) and alopecia areata. Right now, many of the most effective treatment offerings for AD are done via JAK inhibitors, but are largely unsafe, which requires patients to use less effective drugs. Rezpeg has shown competitive efficacy with a clean safety profile and, importantly, strong durability and response deepening in maintenance data, along with infrequent dosing (potentially quarterly), positioning it as a best-in-class option for patients who fail first-line biologics. The AD market is large, with over 80 million patients suffering from the disease globally, generating >$50 billion in sales. If Nektar is able to show positive data in its P3 trial, then peak sales could be anywhere from $3 - $5 billion against a current EV of just ~$2 billion. Similar to our ABVX trade, the ideal scenario would be a buyout with commercialization coming second.

MTY Food Group (MTY.TO)(MTYFF)

MTY is a double-edged restaurant play that, on one end, we view it as an improving restaurant franchisor in what was an abysmal year for the restaurant industry. On the other hand, because so many restaurants saw deteriorating fundamentals and thus, steep stock price declines in 2025, private equity has been circling the drain to scoop up improving assets at depressed valuations. We flagged this in our 6 Themes for 2026 report last December.

MTY has been an asset with improving fundamentals and garnered potential attraction from buyers. Management reaffirmed that they are still in these talks per their last earnings call, where rumors have suggested that a buyout could fetch >$50 CAD.

You can read our original note on the opportunity here.

Disclaimer

Disclaimer: This document is not an offer to invest with Cedar Grove Capital Management, LLC (“CGCM” or the “firm”).

The statements of the investment objectives are statements of objectives only. They are not projections of expected performance nor guarantees of anticipated investment results. Actual performance and results may vary substantially from the stated objectives. Performance returns are calculated by Interactive Brokers.

An investment with the firm involves a high degree of risk and is suitable only for sophisticated investors. Investors should be prepared to suffer losses of their entire investments.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Investing involves risks which clients should be prepared to bear, including but not limited to partial or complete loss of principal originally invested. Investing in small and microcap companies can result in additional volatility and higher risk due to comparatively low market capitalization, more sensitivity to economic and market conditions, and more limited managerial and financial resources.

In addition, small companies typically trade in lower volume, making them more difficult to purchase or sell at the desired time and price or in the desired amount. Please refer to Form ADV Part 2 brochure for more information about Cedar Grove Capital Management and its personnel.

data.title: Commodities: Oil Surges Amid Hormuz Blockade Threat
data.description:
By Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist and Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy

Energy markets opened higher after the US threatened to block traffic through the Strait of Hormuz following the failure of weekend talks with Iran

Energy – Oil prices surge amid Hormuz blockade threat

Oil markets rallied sharply on Monday after US-Iran talks collapsed over the weekend. ICE Brent jumped more than 9% in early trade, while NYMEX WTI pushed above $105/bbl. In response, the US military plans to implement a blockade of all maritime traffic entering and exiting Iranian ports from 10:00am Monday Washington time, while allowing vessels not calling at Iranian ports to continue transiting Hormuz. Despite this, two fuel tankers attempted to exit the Gulf via routes close to Iran’s coastline, marking the first such movements since the blockade was announced.

European gas prices also surged. Front-month TTF futures climbed nearly 18% to intraday highs above EUR51/MWh. The breakdown in peace talks has revived concerns over the conflict, now in its sixth week, while renewed US threats to block Hormuz have intensified fears of near-term supply tightness. Around 20% of global LNG supply has been disrupted, with LNG exports through Hormuz already suspended for more than a month. European gas prices have been up over 50% since the US and Israel first struck Iran in late February.

Positioning data points to growing uncertainty. Speculators reduced their net long in ICE Brent by 5,583 lots to 424,270 lots as of last Tuesday, driven by a 4,525-lot fall in gross longs. By contrast, speculators increased their net long in NYMEX WTI by 7,121 lots over the week, taking it to 137,838 lots.

US drilling activity remains subdued. Baker Hughes data shows the US oil rig count unchanged at 411 as of 10 April, as price volatility and weaker margins continue to weigh on investment. Total rigs fell by three to 545, leaving the count 38 rigs below year-ago levels.

Looking ahead, attention turns to OPEC’s monthly market report due later on Monday, which should provide updated guidance on supply balances amid the escalating geopolitical risks.

Metals – Aluminium jumps to four-year high

Aluminium prices climbed to a four-year high as rising geopolitical risks in the Middle East intensified concerns over shipments and production. US President Donald Trump’s move to block the Strait of Hormuz has raised the risk of further disruption to metal flows, with LME aluminium up as much as 2% on the session. The market is particularly exposed, given that the Middle East accounts for around 9% of global aluminium output and is a key supplier to Europe. Any sustained disruption to shipping through Hormuz would tighten availability and support regional premiums, especially as inventories remain relatively lean. Elevated energy prices are adding to the upside pressure for aluminium, reinforcing cost support for smelters at a time when power markets remain highly volatile.

Other industrial metals traded weaker after the failure of US-Iran negotiations. The sharp rise in oil and gas prices is reviving concerns over the global growth outlook, weighing on demand expectations for more cyclical metals.

Meanwhile, the LME cash/three-month aluminium spread has surged into deep backwardation, widening to $91.5/t – the strongest since 2007 – from a $12/t contango at the onset of the conflict.

CFTC data points to more cautious positioning across industrial metals. Speculators trimmed their net long in COMEX copper by 488 lots to 38,804 lots as of 7 April, as increases in both gross longs (+1,758 lots) and gross shorts (+2,246 lots) signalled rising two-way risk. In silver, net longs were cut by a further 777 lots for a second consecutive week, falling to 10,039 lots, driven by a reduction in gross longs.

Agriculture – Wheat edges lower on supply-driven pressures

CBOT wheat extended losses for a third consecutive session on Friday, ending the week down around 4% – its steepest weekly decline since June 2025. Improving US weather conditions and expectations of ample supply weighed on prices, with forecast rainfall across the southern plains and the USDA’s outlook for higher global availability reinforcing oversupply concerns. Money managers shifted back to a net-short position, with shorts exceeding longs by 5,633 lots over the week, driven largely by a sharp reduction in gross longs. Sentiment was further eased by reduced concerns over fertiliser supply disruptions following a temporary US-Iran ceasefire.

Across other grains, positioning also softened. Speculative net longs in CBOT corn fell for a second consecutive week, declining by 49,342 lots to 218,632 lots as of 7 April, largely due to long liquidation. Net long positions in soybeans dropped by 23,777 lots to 189,630 lots, reflecting a reduction in gross longs amid ongoing trade tensions with China.

Meanwhile, India’s urea production is expected to normalise from Friday, as improved natural gas availability allows previously idle plants to resume operations. Output had fallen sharply in March – by around 800kt from typical monthly levels above 2.6mt – due to gas supply constraints and maintenance shutdowns. The government has since increased gas allocations to the fertiliser sector to around 95% of average consumption, up from 70-75%. However, risks remain, with the US-Iran ceasefire appearing fragile following the failure of talks over the weekend in Islamabad.

data.title: TSMC: The AI Silicon Shortage Is About To Get Worse
data.description:
When I started writing here on Seeking Alpha, my very first articles were centered around one idea. That this AI supercycle was going to test the limits of physics, I didn't realize back then how deep this rabbit hole would actually go. It started as a thesis on AI data center power shortages but has turned into a full research project.

I have learned a lot since I started researching the AI supply chain and sharing that work here, and a lot of the credit for that goes to Seeking Alpha and their editors, who have helped me along the way with every piece I have published. I wouldn't have learned this much if I weren't publishing here. This platform is basically peer-reviewed journaling for me, and every article has pushed the research a layer deeper.

So anyway, over the last couple of weeks, my research has been converging on one point. The sheer scale of AI demand, and the fact that all of it is arriving at once, has created a bottleneck at the very base of the supply chain. We have a power shortage because the grid physically cannot support the load growth coming from AI data centers. We have a huge HBM shortage, which I covered in detail in my Micron piece. And if you read my latest Intel article, we have a CPU shortage, too.

So, shortages everywhere across the supply chain.

And every single one of these components, except electricity, is silicon. It is all semiconductor wafers. GPUs, HBM dies, CPUs, networking ASICs—it doesn't matter. They all funnel down into the same physical process. And basically, one company has a monopoly on that process.

Which brings us to Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM). They report Q1 2026 earnings on April 16th, a couple of days from now, and I will be watching this print very closely. I believe we are about to have a huge shortage of 2nm and 3nm advanced chips. A multi-year waitlist kind of shortage.

Because Everything Is Sold Out

If I had to pick a single factor that is going to drive TSMC's stock over the next three years, it would be that TSMC has become a monopoly on the only production process that can build every single chip that matters for AI. And everything else in this piece is me trying to convince you that the market has not yet fully repriced the stock to reflect what that actually means.

Because the demand trajectory is compounding every year at a very high pace, and the supply to resolve it has a lead time of four to five years. Their 3nm nodes are going to run above 100% effective utilization in the second half of this year. You can go above 100% of theoretical capacity by pushing existing lines harder, rerouting process layers, and pulling every last wafer out of tools that were never designed to run this hot. They are doing all of that right now. And it is still not enough.

At the same time, N2 entered mass production in Q4 of last year. Fab 20 in Hsinchu and Fab 22 in Kaohsiung started at the same time, which is the first time TSMC has ever ramped a new node at two sites simultaneously. That’s why both are fully sold out for 2026 and nearly sold out for 2027.

At the end of 2025, they had roughly 40k to 50k wafers per month of 2nm capacity and about 150k WPM of 3nm. By the end of 2026, they are trying to ramp 2nm to 80k to 130k WPM, and 3nm is targeted at 180k to 220k WPM. It sounds like a lot of wafers. Until you look at the demand. Apple has locked up more than 50% of the 2nm capacity for the A20, M6, and R2. That's before NVIDIA Corporation's (NVDA) migration of their chips to 2 nm, which starts in 2027, and not to mention AMD’s demand for MI400 on N2.

Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) has pre-reserved a huge slice of 2nm for Alphabet Inc.'s (GOOG) (GOOGL) TPU and OpenAI. I am actually writing a separate piece right now on Broadcom and the position that they have built for themselves because of the capacity they secured from TSMC years in advance. So make sure to follow and stay tuned for that one.

An important thing to note here is that I also wrote in my Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) piece. HBM4 base dies alone would add hundreds of millions of units to TSMC's N3/N5 load. Because the HBM4 base die is made on advanced FinFET on N3 or N5. So every HBM4 stack going into a Blackwell Ultra or a Rubin is basically eating TSMC's advanced node wafers twice.

Once for the GPU compute die, once for the HBM base die on it. And every single Rubin GPU needs 8 or more of them stacked on it. You are basically asking N3 to absorb not just the GPUs but also the memory dies that go next to the GPUs.

CoWoS Is Also Overcrowded

And even once you get the wafer, you still have to package it. CoWoS advanced packaging is the pipe that connects the GPU die to the HBM stacks. TSM has ramped up CoWoS from roughly 35k wafers per month in late 2024 to 75 to 80k by the end of last year and is targeting 120 to 130k by the end of 2026. And it is still not enough.

Morgan Stanley has global CoWoS demand at roughly 1 million wafers in 2026. TSMC will ship, let's call it 1.3 million at the 120k WPM run rate by the end of the year, so nominally the numbers look close.

But if you look at the bookings:

The math tells you the top 3 alone consume 85% of the output. Google already had to cut its 2026 TPU production target from 4 million units to 3 million because it could not get enough CoWoS slots.

What I'm Actually Watching On April 16

Okay, so with all of that as context, let's get into what I will be looking for in the upcoming print. TSMC has already guided Q1 to $34.6 billion to $35.8 billion in revenue, or about 38% YoY at the midpoint, with a gross margin of 63 to 65%. Consensus sits at roughly 35 billion. I think they will most likely beat it. But here is what I am most interested in:

First, the revenue guide for Q2 and any update to the full year. They guided 2026 to grow “close to 30%” in US dollar terms back in January. Since then, we've had Jensen's GTC keynote, where he casually dropped $1 trillion in orders for Blackwell and Vera Rubin through 2027. We have had Nvidia taking over as their number one customer, from Apple, by revenue. And we have had Broadcom's AI revenue running at a $33 billion annualized rate with a $73 billion backlog, all of which has to be built somewhere, and that somewhere is TSMC. If the 2026 guide does not get revised upward, I will be genuinely surprised. If it gets revised upward by a meaningful amount, that is the bullish signal.

Second would be the gross margins, but specifically the Q2 and full-year guide. The Q1 guide of 63 to 65% already bakes in the overseas ramp dilution and the early N2 drag. What I care about is the next quarter. If TSMC guides Q2 flat or up, it means the pricing power on N3P is offsetting the Arizona drag and that Apple has most likely swallowed the 2nm wafer price hike.

If Q2 guidance implies sequential margin compression, then I am looking for a valid explanation from them. If it's because of the overseas ramp, then that’s a good sign it will start to normalize in the next couple of quarters. But I do not expect this to happen, because when N3 pricing is going up 5 to 10% and N2 is booked out to 2028, the mix effect alone should outrun the dilution. It will be the single most important line in the release.

Third, and this is where you have to read between the lines on the call, I want to hear management talk about utilization and the booking curve. Last quarter, the CEO used the language that “focused more on short-term output” for N2. This quarter, I expect that language to get stronger, not weaker.

Specifically, I am listening for

  • Any update on how much of 2027 N2 is already allocated.

  • How they talk about N3X and whether the premium variants are getting pulled forward or not.

If management signals that 2027 N2 is sold out, that would be a huge forward indicator because it means every new AI chip design decision being made right now for the 2028 product is either getting denied or being pushed to N3 with a price hike.

What This Demand Would Look Like Financially

I have walked you through the supply/demand math. Now let's talk about what all of this is actually worth. At the current price, TSM trades at roughly 25x forward P/E and 14x forward EV/EBITDA, with a PEG ratio of 0.83 on a forward non-GAAP basis.

I think you can safely say that TSMC is in the range of fairly valued to slightly undervalued. Because they are growing twice as fast as the sector, at margins five to seven times higher, with a forward P/E that is only 15% above the sector median. On a PEG basis, it is trading at a 38% discount to the sector median.

TSMC did $122.4 billion in 2025 revenue, up 35.9% YoY in USD terms. They guided 2026 to grow “close to 30%” in US dollar terms despite FX headwinds, and I think that guide may get revised upward on April 16th for reasons I walked through earlier.

So, here is my revenue forecast through 2030:

A few things to note here. Their own guide was 25% CAGR in USD terms for the 5-year period starting in 2024. And AI accelerators specifically compounding in the mid-50% range for 2024 to 2029.

My revenue forecast works out to a CAGR of roughly 27%, which is slightly above their number. The reason for that is that Nvidia’s GTC orders of $1 trillion through 2027 were not in their long-term guide when they first issued it. Moreover, Broadcom's custom ASIC ramp for OpenAI and Anthropic is added to what was modeled, and there are 5 to 10% price hikes on N3 and N5 already in motion.

Now, if you apply a normalized 50% net margin on that FY27 revenue number of $206 billion, you get net income of roughly $103 billion. Divide that by 25.9 billion diluted shares (about 5.2 billion ADR equivalents), and you are looking at FY27 EPS of around $19.8 per ADR.

The stock right now is trading at just 10x FY27 earnings. And keep in mind that Nvidia trades at 28x forward earnings. Broadcom trades at 31x.

What Could Go Wrong

And I think the reason for this valuation discount is a Taiwan-specific risk. A Taiwan Strait event is a very low probability but high risk and unmodelable. This risk is what keeps them from reaching Nvidia or Broadcom levels and, honestly, probably always will. But in my opinion, the valuation still has enough room to run because of the sheer scale of demand.

The risk to them is not the competition. Samsung's advanced node yields are still not competitive. Intel 18A is a credible process for Intel's own products, but not a threat to its external foundry business.

The biggest risk apart from a Taiwan Strait event is a what-if question. What if the AI capex cycle breaks and hyperscalers are forced to a capex cut because of a macro recession or financial crisis? Although the probability of that is very low, there is still a risk associated with many of these AI buildout investment theses.

Final Thoughts

On April 16, I will be watching the revenue guide, the Q2 margin guide, the language around N2 bookings, and the CoWoS ramp update. If management does what I expect them to do, which is raise the full-year revenue outlook, hold margin guidance flat, and reiterate that the capacity is booked out beyond 2028, then the stock is materially undervalued at the current valuation.

data.title: Dynagas LNG: Mispricing Risk, Undervaluing Cash Flow
data.description:
The "No-Free-Lunch" Disclaimer

Investing in international shipping is rarely about smooth sailing. When you mix in Russian sanctions and Middle Eastern turmoil, it’s more like trying to sail through a tornado. Right now, Dynagas LNG Partners (DLNG) is trading at a valuation that suggests the market expects the ship to hit an iceberg any minute.

But as the old saying goes, price is what you pay, value is what you get. If you can stomach a bit of geopolitical theater, the math here starts to look less like a tragedy and more like a very profitable accounting exercise.

The Geopolitical Fog: Real or Mirage?

The bear case is easy to spot: DLNG has one-third of its fleet tied to Russian gas. By January 2027, the sanction hammer is expected to fall. On top of that, the Middle East is a powder keg, and new, shiny vessels are hitting the water from Korean yards, making older fleets look like relics. A previous author covered these risks well back in March.

Risks:

  • The Russian "Iceberg": New EU rules might ban DLNG from moving Yamal gas anywhere by 2027. Since these contracts are the backbone of the partnership's income, we’re talking about a potential total blackout for a third of the fleet.

  • The Mirage of High Rates: Geopolitical chaos in the Middle East might spike rates today, but it creates a messy, unpredictable market. In the long run, this turmoil usually leads to a vessel oversupply that makes securing stable, long-term charters much harder.

  • Old Tech, New Crowds: We are holding an aging fleet in a market about to be swamped by nearly 100 high-tech new-builds by 2026. Our older ice-class ships risk becoming "relics" that struggle to compete with more efficient newcomers.

However, the market has a habit of over-discounting "scary" headlines while ignoring "boring" cash flows. These aren't just any ships; they are high-spec, ice-class vessels. They aren't easily replaced, and in today's uncertain energy climate, they remain in high demand.

The Financial Engine: Deleveraging Is the Strategy

In 2025 (Dynagas LNG Partners LP Report 2025), DLNG produced $90 million in cash from operations—almost identical to the $92 million from the year before. It’s what they did with that money that matters.

Instead of chasing "growth" (which in shipping usually means buying overpriced ships at the top of the cycle), management is quietly tidying the house. In the last year alone:

  • $55 million went to redeeming preferred shares.
  • $45 million was used to hack away at the debt.
  • $16 million went to dividends (common & preferred).
  • $1 million was used for buybacks.

They spent $117 million improving the balance sheet, while their cash only dipped by $27 million. They are effectively buying the company back from the banks. Every dollar of debt retired is a win for us, the common shareholders, because it kills the "interest tax" we pay to Wall Street.

The "Melting Ice Cube" Valuation

Critics call DLNG a "melting ice cube" because the fleet is getting older. I call it a cigar butt. If we assume the partnership never buys another ship and simply runs the current fleet into the ground, what’s left?

If we take a conservative scrap value of $20 million per vessel, the 6-ship fleet is worth $120 million just as recycled steel in five years. Since many of their contracts run past that 5-year mark, this is a "worst-case" floor. Anything that happens after Year 5 is just a very sweet, very juicy cherry on top.

The Debt Math: A Path to Zero

The current "pile of bills" includes $277 million in debt and $73 million in preferred equity, costing about 6% in interest.

Assuming stable operating conditions, we assume the debt repayment is constant at $75 million per year; look what happens to the cash left over for the common shareholders. As the debt disappears, the interest we don't pay flows straight to the bottom line (and since the tax rate is 0%, it stays there).
